title: JavaScript
purpose: Master the browser and Node.js language used across modern frontend and full-stack products.
outcomes: Explain JavaScript concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important JavaScript features
Implement, test and debug practical JavaScript solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of JavaScript before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Values, coercion and operators
topics.definition: A type describes the values an expression may hold and the operations permitted on them. A variable or binding gives a value a name within a scope.
topics.whyItMatters: Types and bindings make state explicit, prevent invalid operations and let readers or tools reason about how data changes.
whenToUse: Represent domain facts with the narrowest useful type
Use immutable bindings for values that should not change
Validate data when it crosses from an untrusted external source
whenNotToUse: Using a broad string or any type for every domain value
Introducing mutable shared state when a value can be passed explicitly
Relying on implicit coercion where correctness matters
internalWorking: A declaration creates a binding in a scope
Primitive values are represented directly while object variables usually hold references
The runtime resolves the binding, evaluates the expression and applies conversion rules
Static languages reject incompatible operations before execution; dynamic languages check during execution
tradeoffs: Narrow types improve safety but require deliberate modeling
Mutation can simplify local algorithms but complicates shared reasoning
Implicit conversion is concise but can hide defects
commonMistakes: Comparing values using identity when content equality is required
Ignoring numeric overflow or floating-point precision
Confusing an uninitialized value with an intentionally empty value
topics.practicalExample: Model an assessment score as a bounded number rather than a free-form string, reject values outside 0-100, and derive eligibility instead of storing a second mutable flag.
topics.code:
const rankStudents = (students) =>
  [...students]
    .filter(({ score }) => score >= 70)
    .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score);

topics.title: Arrays, objects and destructuring
topics.definition: Object-oriented programming groups state with the behavior that protects and transforms it. Encapsulation protects invariants, polymorphism lets callers depend on contracts, and inheritance is one possible reuse mechanism.
topics.whyItMatters: It helps model long-lived domain concepts whose state must remain valid while implementations evolve behind stable interfaces.
whenToUse: Model entities such as Student, Assessment and Submission with clear invariants
Hide changeable implementation details behind an interface
Use polymorphism when several implementations honor the same behavioral contract
whenNotToUse: Creating classes that only hold unrelated utility functions
Using inheritance only to reuse code
Building deep hierarchies where composition expresses the relationship more clearly
internalWorking: An object has identity, state and behavior exposed through methods
A class defines construction and method dispatch rules
Dynamic dispatch selects an overriding implementation using the runtime object type
Encapsulation keeps invalid updates behind methods that enforce invariants
tradeoffs: Stable boundaries versus extra abstractions
Inheritance offers substitution but tightly couples parent and child
Mutable objects are convenient but increase coordination and testing cost
commonMistakes: Calling fields private without actually protecting invariants
Confusing overloading with overriding
Using instanceof chains instead of polymorphic behavior
topics.practicalExample: Assessment.submit(answer) can reject submissions after the deadline. The caller cannot directly mutate the internal status and bypass that rule.

topics.title: Promises and async/await
topics.definition: A promise represents the eventual completion or failure of asynchronous work. async/await provides sequential syntax while preserving promise-based execution.
topics.whyItMatters: Network and timer operations finish later; promises provide one composable value for success, failure and chaining.
whenToUse: Coordinate API requests and other non-blocking operations
Run independent operations concurrently with an explicit aggregate
Propagate cancellation and timeout signals
whenNotToUse: Awaiting independent operations one by one
Creating a promise around an already promise-based API
Ignoring rejection or component cleanup
internalWorking: Calling an async function immediately returns a promise
Await suspends that function, not the JavaScript thread
Promise reactions enter the microtask queue
The event loop runs microtasks after the current stack before the next task
tradeoffs: Readable sequential syntax versus hidden concurrency if awaits are misplaced
Promise.all is fast-fail and concurrent but needs a partial-failure policy
Cancellation is cooperative rather than automatic
commonMistakes: Forgetting to return or await a promise
Using array forEach with async callbacks
Updating UI from a stale request
topics.practicalExample: Load profile and roadmap concurrently with Promise.all, pass one AbortSignal to both, and show a recoverable error if either required request fails.

    .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score);

topics.title: Map, Set and array transformations
topics.definition: A stream or functional pipeline describes a sequence of transformations such as filter, map, group and reduce without manually controlling each iteration.
topics.whyItMatters: It separates what result is required from low-level iteration and supports composable data processing.
whenToUse: Transform collections through clear stateless steps
Express filtering, grouping and aggregation
Process values lazily when the runtime supports it
whenNotToUse: Pipelines with hidden side effects
Complex branching that is clearer as ordinary control flow
Parallel execution without proving the work is large, independent and thread-safe
internalWorking: Intermediate operations build a pipeline and a terminal operation triggers evaluation
Each element flows through compatible stages
Lazy evaluation can fuse work and stop early
Closures capture surrounding values, which should remain effectively immutable
tradeoffs: Concise composition versus harder step-through debugging
Lazy evaluation can reduce work but hides when execution starts
Parallel streams add coordination overhead and ordering concerns
commonMistakes: Mutating external state inside map or filter

topics.definition: Object-oriented programming groups state with the behavior that protects and transforms it. Encapsulation protects invariants, polymorphism lets callers depend on contracts, and inheritance is one possible reuse mechanism.

Encapsulation keeps invalid updates behind methods that enforce invariants

Inheritance offers substitution but tightly couples parent and child
Mutable objects are convenient but increase coordination and testing cost

Using instanceof chains instead of polymorphic behavior

topics.title: Workers, streams and cancellation
topics.definition: A stream or functional pipeline describes a sequence of transformations such as filter, map, group and reduce without manually controlling each iteration.
topics.whyItMatters: It separates what result is required from low-level iteration and supports composable data processing.
whenToUse: Transform collections through clear stateless steps
Express filtering, grouping and aggregation
Process values lazily when the runtime supports it
whenNotToUse: Pipelines with hidden side effects
Complex branching that is clearer as ordinary control flow
Parallel execution without proving the work is large, independent and thread-safe
internalWorking: Intermediate operations build a pipeline and a terminal operation triggers evaluation
Each element flows through compatible stages
Lazy evaluation can fuse work and stop early
Closures capture surrounding values, which should remain effectively immutable
tradeoffs: Concise composition versus harder step-through debugging
Lazy evaluation can reduce work but hides when execution starts
Parallel streams add coordination overhead and ordering concerns
commonMistakes: Mutating external state inside map or filter
Reusing a consumed stream
Creating a pipeline that is less readable than a loop

topics.title: Promise scheduling
topics.definition: A promise represents the eventual completion or failure of asynchronous work. async/await provides sequential syntax while preserving promise-based execution.
topics.whyItMatters: Network and timer operations finish later; promises provide one composable value for success, failure and chaining.
whenToUse: Coordinate API requests and other non-blocking operations
Run independent operations concurrently with an explicit aggregate
Propagate cancellation and timeout signals
whenNotToUse: Awaiting independent operations one by one
Creating a promise around an already promise-based API
Ignoring rejection or component cleanup
internalWorking: Calling an async function immediately returns a promise
Await suspends that function, not the JavaScript thread
Promise reactions enter the microtask queue
The event loop runs microtasks after the current stack before the next task
tradeoffs: Readable sequential syntax versus hidden concurrency if awaits are misplaced
Promise.all is fast-fail and concurrent but needs a partial-failure policy
Cancellation is cooperative rather than automatic
commonMistakes: Forgetting to return or await a promise
Using array forEach with async callbacks
Updating UI from a stale request
topics.practicalExample: Load profile and roadmap concurrently with Promise.all, pass one AbortSignal to both, and show a recoverable error if either required request fails.

topics.title: Event-loop order
topics.definition: Object-oriented programming groups state with the behavior that protects and transforms it. Encapsulation protects invariants, polymorphism lets callers depend on contracts, and inheritance is one possible reuse mechanism.
topics.whyItMatters: It helps model long-lived domain concepts whose state must remain valid while implementations evolve behind stable interfaces.
whenToUse: Model entities such as Student, Assessment and Submission with clear invariants
Hide changeable implementation details behind an interface
Use polymorphism when several implementations honor the same behavioral contract
whenNotToUse: Creating classes that only hold unrelated utility functions
Using inheritance only to reuse code
Building deep hierarchies where composition expresses the relationship more clearly
internalWorking: An object has identity, state and behavior exposed through methods
A class defines construction and method dispatch rules
Dynamic dispatch selects an overriding implementation using the runtime object type
Encapsulation keeps invalid updates behind methods that enforce invariants
tradeoffs: Stable boundaries versus extra abstractions
Inheritance offers substitution but tightly couples parent and child
Mutable objects are convenient but increase coordination and testing cost
commonMistakes: Calling fields private without actually protecting invariants
Confusing overloading with overriding
Using instanceof chains instead of polymorphic behavior
topics.practicalExample: Assessment.submit(answer) can reject submissions after the deadline. The caller cannot directly mutate the internal status and bypass that rule.

    .filter(({ score }) => score >= 70)
